pages.text:
BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014
CASE STUDY
BLOCK PERIODIZATION AND MIXING DIFFERENT GOALS
Note: this is a copy of the anonymized client intake form from one of my clients with my client
evaluation at the end.

The more information you provide, the better I will be able to assist you. If you have
any questions with regards to this form, please do not hesitate to contact me. You
can use whichever measurement system (e.g. metric, imperial) you prefer to fill in
this form. Just state which unit you used (e.g. cm, kg, lb, inch).

•
Full legal name: Greg
•
Full billing address:
•
Age: 32
•
Height: 5,10in
•
Weight: 78kg
•
Body fat percentage (plus measurement technique): 11-13% Bio-impedance
inbody570 machine
•
Years of training experience: 10 years
For the section below, please give your current maximum abilities in weight times
reps, e.g. 250 lb x 6.
•
Bench press: 107.5k x 1
•
Squat (excluding body weight): 120k x 1
•
Chin-up (excluding body weight): 25k x 3
•
Overhead press: 60k x 5

What are your fitness goals? To build muscle and strength. Keep body fat in low teens
while putting on muscle and getting strong. Started Olympic Weightlifting 2 months
ago and want to improve OL while putting on muscle mass. Main goal is hypertrophy
while keeping body fat low. Also want to improve on the 3 powerlifts.
headings: BLOCK PERIODIZATION AND MIXING DIFFERENT GOALS

pages.text:
BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014

How often in a week would you be prepared to train? Note that no session should last
longer than an hour; 2 sessions is maintenance, 3 is doable and twice daily would
be the absolute maximum at which more is no longer better.
Can train 6 days per week, except a Friday. Can train once or twice per day as works
in gym. Can train usually between 10am-4pm.

What is your occupation? You may be general here, e.g. student/manager/personal
trainer/manual labor/doctor. The aim is to characterize your circadian rhythm,
stress and activity level.
Personal trainer working in gym. So on feet for most of the day. Start work at 6am
most mornings and finish at around 8pm, but this varies. One day off per week.

Please list any pathologies and injuries (disabilities, allergies, illnesses, syndromes,
disorders etc.) you have or had: in the past have injured rotator cuff and this acts up
every now and then but in never more than just an annoyance. Had/has Crohn's
disease but is now medication free and has been for 6 months. Does not need to avoid
any foods anymore either.

Do you plan to use any PEDs while under my supervision? If so, please list them:
No

Note that if you answer 'NO' to any of the questions below, I will not be able to take
you on as a client.

BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014
•
I declare that, while Menno Henselmans is my personal trainer, all the substances I
use are obtained and used in accordance with their governing laws: YES
•
I forego all rights to hold Menno Henselmans liable for any damages caused in
relation to following his advice as my trainer: YES

At this point, you may choose to return this form to me to obtain a price
quote before filling in the rest. Please return the filled in form to
mennohenselmans@bayesianbodybuilding.com and attach at least two full-body
pictures of yourself in clear lighting, preferably including a back double biceps and
front relaxed pose.

Activity level: please mark one of the fields below or delete the rest.
•
Sedentary (e.g. desk job)
•
Somewhat active (e.g. doing groceries or walking your dog but otherwise sedentary)
•
Active (e.g. on your feet much of the day) Y
•
Very active (e.g. involved in manual labor)
Stress level: please mark one of the fields below or delete the rest.
•
Stress-free (e.g. on holiday)
•
Only occasional/mild stress (e.g. student not during exam period)
•
Average stress (e.g. full-time work with deadlines and commuting) Y
•
High stress (e.g. very high-paced work environment with great responsibility)
If you answered yes to the question on PED usage, you can leave the section below
blank.
•
Wrist circumference: 18.5cm
•
Ankle circumference: 24.5cm
•
How much did you weigh before you started lifting and what was your height at that
point? Not sure, lowest weight was 126lbs after being diagnosed with Crohns at age
20 at height 5ft 10in.
•
Were you heavy as a baby? Was 1 month premature and weighed 6lbs. Gained weight
well after that and was a chubby toddler.

pages.text:
BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014
•
Please attach a picture to this form in which the back of your hand is clearly visible
(see example image below).

What are the lightest barbell weight plates you have access to? 1.25k

What are the smallest weight increments between the sets of dumbbells you have
access to? 2k

Do you have access to…
a squat cage or rack? Y
a 45° hyperextension bench? N
a reverse hyper machine? N
a glute-ham raise? N
a standing calf raise machine? Y
a dip/chin-up belt? Y
a leg curl machine and if so, is it seated, lying or standing? Seated only, but use leg ext
to do standing leg curls.
a pair of knee wraps? No but could buy
a seated calf raise where you push your body up (closed chain: see example image
below)? No but have used smith bench to replicate.

Please list all the supplements you are currently taking: Fish oil 2-6 per day, Multi
Vit, Zinc, Magnesium, Vitamin B complex, Vitamin C, Creatine

BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014

Please describe or attach your current diet plan in detail: my macros are currently;
On training days (5 days)- Cal- 3115, Pro-140-150g, Fat- 115g min-175g max, Carbs-
240g min- 380g max
One low day of- Cal- 2100, Pro- 140-150g, Fat 115g, Carbs- 125g
One day of- Cal- 2585, Pro- 140-150g, Fat 115g min- 170g max, Carbs- 125g min-
250g max
My diet is very varied and don't dislike any foods really.
I reversed from about 2000kcal to get to this point over 5 months. Spent 2 years
dieting to get down from 96kg which is the heaviest I have ever been at about 22% bf
(measured by nutritionist with calipers)

Please describe or attach your current training program in detail:
I lift weights 5 days per week on a heavy/light split. Legs 4 days per week with a push
pull split for upper body. One day for arms. I do Olympic lifts at start of workout
Snatch 2 days, Clean & Jerk 2 days. Strength and hypertrophy work for the rest of the
workout. Reps range from 1-5 for Olympic lifts. 5-15 for other work. Volume is legs
one push one pull exercise each day (not including Olympic lifts) 3-5 sets. Upper
push/pull 1-2 of each and isolation exercises for each 3-5 sets.
I do cardio/HIIT on one day and rest on a Friday.
Have been doing this for 4 weeks. Before this did a 16 week powerlifting program.

Are there any times at which you are unable or unwilling to train? If so, please list
them: cannot train 6am-10am or 5pm-8pm usually. I cannot train on a Friday.
------------------------

BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014
Client assessment
This is a case study with a situation that you every PT will almost surely encounter: a client
having several different goals. The key here is in the goals section:

"To build muscle and strength. Keep body fat in low teens while putting on muscle and
getting strong. Started Olympic Weightlifting 2 months ago and want to improve OL while
putting on muscle mass. Main goal is hypertrophy while keeping body fat low. Also want to
improve on the 3 powerlifts."

Building muscle and strength simultaneously is very feasible as we've seen. Keeping one's
body fat percentage in the low teens is also very doable. In fact, we've seen it's advisable
even for optimal nutrient partitioning. The problem arises in sentence 3, combining strength
and size with Olympic weightlifting. Then at the end powerlifting is added to the mix.

This is problematic, because Olympic weightlifting and bodybuilding do not match very well.
Even aside from the potential architectural interference between muscular hypertrophy and
power development, the Olympic lifts are extremely technical and will thus require a great
deal of practice. Since the Olympic lifts are poor mass building exercises (e.g. few dynamic
contractions aside from the squatting, partial ROM, terrible resistance curves), combining a
high volume of these lifts will not allow for a sufficient training volume of hypertrophy
focused training without inducing overtraining.

This is true in general, but it's especially true for Greg, because he is an intermediate, close
to advanced trainee. Judging by his skeletal frame structure, he should not yet be near his
muscular potential, but he's certainly past the level where general strength training improves
fitness in every regard.

When I asked Greg what the #1 issue in his training was, he replied, "The problem is that I
do not put on any decent muscle while keeping my body fat low. Especially in my legs."

The solution then is to incorporate both block and undulating periodization. Since Greg is
not competing, there is no need to peak and thus no need for linear periodization. Here's
how this periodization structure looks on the macro level:

Blocks 1-2: Hypertrophy & Strength - Double undulating periodization - Lean bulking
Mesocycle 1,3,5, etc.: 75%
headings: Blocks 1-2: Hypertrophy & Strength – Double undulating periodization – Lean bulking

pages.text:
BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014
Mesocycle 2,4,6, etc.: 85%

These blocks will have programs that are very much like what you've learned in this course
on optimal program design for bodybuilding during a lean bulk. The Powerlifts or close
variants, like Snatch grip deficit deadlifts instead of conventional pulling for the sake of full
ROM training, can easily be incorporated to improve Powerlifting performance as well.

Olympic lifting is best kept at a maintenance level. Technique and power work are fine to
include in general, as the fatigue from this is minimal, and they should definitely suffice to
maintain strength in non-advanced Olympic lifters. I generally have clients do some of this
before their general strength training program, as then it dual-serves as the warm-up for the
training session. Adding technique/power training sessions can also serve as 'cardio' when
that's needed.

Block 3: Powerlifting & Olympic Weightlifting - Cutting

Here you would specialize in Powerlifting and/or Olympic Weightlifting. Diet wise, it's
optimal to use these periods to cut, as strength and particularly relative strength can be
improved enormously even in an energy deficit. Not much muscle growth will occur over
the long term in these blocks anyway, so this is a good time to achieve fat loss.

Note that the ratio of different blocks can be adjusted to fit with the person's goals. The
lower Olympic Weightlifting and Powerlifting are valued, the fewer training specific blocks
they should receive. If Greg really wants to do well in all 3 strength sports, he will at some
point have to incorporate conjugate block periodization where the Olympic lifts and the
Powerlifts are all incorporated in every macrocycle at at least maintenance level.

Cardio should generally be avoided during all blocks, because it can interfere with the
development of all 3 strength sports. It also shouldn't be needed due to the high training
volume necessitated by practicing the Powerlifts, the Olympic lifts and getting in enough
volume for an optimal hypertrophic stimulus.

In the end, it comes down to having the client clearly prioritize his or her goals and
combining compatible training stimuli in the same training blocks. Trying to develop every
quality at the same time is a sure way to obtain mediocre results for all of them.

all_headings: BLOCK PERIODIZATION AND MIXING DIFFERENT GOALS

quote before filling in the rest. Please return the filled in form to
Blocks 1-2: Hypertrophy & Strength – Double undulating periodization – Lean bulking
